summary: Julia Evans explores how individual speakers can give better conference talks and how organizers can build better conferences. She argues that good talks must teach the audience something new in an understandable way, and offers concrete strategies: thinking deeply about your audience, keeping talks short, getting honest feedback, and talking about things you didn't build. She advocates for 'hard talks' that push audience knowledge while remaining accessible to multiple skill levels. For organizers, she suggests lightning talk tracks, pairing speakers for practice, writing desired talk ideas on websites, and actively recruiting speakers from other communities.
key_points: A good talk is one where the audience learns something new or gains a change in perspective — clarity matters more than polish
Short talks (10 minutes) set speakers up for success by forcing them to cut everything that isn't essential
Talks about things you didn't build are more credible and give you a wider range of interesting topics to choose from
Hard, advanced talks are risky but proportionally rewarding — they tell newer audience members what concepts exist and what experts think is important
Conference organizers should actively recruit speakers, suggest talk topics, and provide institutional support rather than passively accepting submissions
money_quotes: After you go once or twice you don't really go to the talks any more – you won't learn anything. Just hang out in the hallway!
I don't mind too much how many times you say 'um' or how nice your slides are.
If you tell someone 'hey, pick an extremely interesting topic and prepare 10 minutes of material on it', they're set up for success. You can't put in irrelevant or boring stuff! There's no time!
Talking about someone else's work is kinda magical because instead of discussing the small subset of software I've built, I get to pick from ALL THE SOFTWARE IN THE UNIVERSE.
It's super important to be able to watch talks that are somewhat above your current level – they tell you what other concepts exist, and what people with more expertise think is important.

People are terrible at figuring out what cool stuff they know. Awful. I often tell someone 'hey you know this thing that is awesome' and they're like 'wait really? doesn't everyone know that? are you sure???'
If you accept truly difficult talks by speakers who can make the content accessible to enough people, then I think you can end up with a conference that people love going to every year.
key_insight: The best conference talks teach the audience something genuinely new in an understandable way, and both speakers and organizers should actively optimize for learning rather than polish.
